content: p In a previous article, we detailed  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  example integrations of motion control and robotics  a . So, what about the workcells and systems surrounding the robotics in their workcells? Various automated systems can serve, move, and enhance a robot to maximize its usefulness.  p   p There are various complexity levels with these peripheral  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  motion  a  systems. The act of  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  machine tending  a  is simpler than assembly   and most  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  assembly  a  tasks are in turn simpler than operations involving conveyance.  p   p The latter represents  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  material handling  a  in the form of pick-and-place functions  as seen below  needing the coordination of all the robot axes and end effector with the conveyor. For such setups, the location of the robot relative to the conveyor, along with a whole host of other parameters, can be optimized in  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  simulation  a  software for minimized arm travel.  p   p  img loading  lazy  decoding  async  class  aligncenter wp-image-27432  src    alt    width  699  height  393      p   p Of course, no discussion of peripheral motion systems that complement robotics would be complete without mentioning seventh-axis systems   also called  robot-transfer units  or RTUs.  p   p Engineers can buy pre-engineered RTUs or build them in-house using motion-system expertise. The simplest RTUs are linear-track pairs bearing platforms to which the robot bolts.  p   p However, many OEMs enlist dedicated integrators for situations where robots on RTUs will perform high-precision jobs. For example, it could be a cutting task, where the design must synchronize the articulation of multiple axes, or moving castings through various machine tools for processing.  p   figure id  attachment_27433  class  wp-caption aligncenter  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27433    div id  attachment_27433  style  width  710px  class  wp-caption aligncenter   img loading  lazy  decoding  async  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27433  class  wp-image-27433  src    alt    width  700  height  394     p id  caption-attachment-27433  class  wp-caption-text  This is a Bishop-Wisecarver seventh-axis solution. Credit  Lisa Eitel  p   div   figure   p The biggest challenge to engineering RTUs is programming them to synchronize with the articulation of the robot arms they carry. The second-biggest challenge is getting RTUs to maintain accurate linear motion over many meters.  p   p Yet other linear motion technologies complement robotic workcells. Here we see  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  PBC Linear s  a   a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  Applied Cobotics cobot feeder  a    an automated rack for a  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  CNC  a  machine tending applications.  p   p More specifically, the part-feeding system delivers and stages parts going into a machining process. The solution has  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  Universal Robots  a   UR  partner certification.  p   p  img loading  lazy  decoding  async  class  aligncenter size-full wp-image-27434  src    alt    width  608  height  1080      p   p The  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  cobot  a  feeder includes a storage and retrieval assembly with a UR force- and power-limited robot pedestal, enclosed steel rack dunnage tower with a 17-tray capacity for holding workpieces to 22.6 kg  50 lb. , and a touchscreen human-machine interface  HMI . It also includes Ethernet IP, Modbus, and standard UR cobot communications.  p   p Leading candidates for such machine-tending peripheral systems are those that are especially complicated. The video clip below shows how machine builder Card-Monroe uses the Applied Cobotics feeder in the production of its complex tufting machines   which can have 50,000 parts.  p   p The feeder helps automate the staging and handling of parts going into CNC machines. By automating this machine tending, Card-Monroe achieved a 78  reduction in labor costs.  p   p  iframe loading  lazy  title  Card Monroe Cobot Feeder Case Study  width  740  height  416  src    frameborder  0  allow  accelerometer  autoplay  clipboard-write  encrypted-media  gyroscope  picture-in-picture  web-share  referrerpolicy  strict-origin-when-cross-origin  allowfullscreen   iframe   p   p Eighth-axis vertical RTUs are another linear-motion solution for robotic workcells. Bishop-Wisecarver s RTU-V is an extension of LoPro RTU-L and -M offerings. Vertical strokes reach 4 m  13.1 ft.  to extend robotic-arm reach in  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  logistics  a ,  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  manufacturing  a ,  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  agriculture  a , and  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  packaging  a .  p   p This kind of eighth-axis RTU is also useful where robotic arms work on oversized assemblies such as rocket tubes, boat hulls, and  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  aerospace  a  structures.  p   p  img loading  lazy  decoding  async  class  aligncenter size-large wp-image-27435  src    sizes  auto,  max-width  1024px  100vw, 1024px  srcset    1024w,   300w,   768w,   1536w,   2048w  alt    width  1024  height  907      p   p Compatible with LoPro components and the DualVee RTU-H, this RTU-V can be mounted vertically to deliver two-axis motion.  p   p One caveat  These need a structural mounting bracket to maintain stability and precision. In other words, they can t just be mounted directly to a factory wall. That RTU-V mounting bracket bolts to the factory floor or to a DualVee RTU-H.  p   figure id  attachment_27436  class  wp-caption aligncenter  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27436    div id  attachment_27436  style  width  1034px  class  wp-caption aligncenter   img loading  lazy  decoding  async  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27436  class  wp-image-27436 size-large  src    sizes  auto,  max-width  1024px  100vw, 1024px  srcset    1024w,   300w,   768w,   1536w,   2048w  alt    width  1024  height  683     p id  caption-attachment-27436  class  wp-caption-text  Eighth-axis systems can support the assembly of oversized assemblies such as aircraft structures. Source  Adobe Stock  p   div   figure   figure id  attachment_27437  class  wp-caption aligncenter  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27437    div id  attachment_27437  style  width  464px  class  wp-caption aligncenter   img loading  lazy  decoding  async  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27437  class  wp-image-27437  src    sizes  auto,  max-width  454px  100vw, 454px  srcset    691w,   300w  alt    width  454  height  333     p id  caption-attachment-27437  class  wp-caption-text  UR software plug-ins allow for conveyor configuration and control.   Source  Universal Robots  p   div   figure   h2 The special case of conveyor tending  h2   p As mentioned, robotic tending of conveyors is maximally challenging.  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  Conveyors  a  involve variable speeds and variable workpiece positions, unless puck and pallet-fixture conveyors that cradle workpieces are used.  p   p Conveyors are motion designs in their own right with belt or power roller, motor, braking, and control elements all needing consideration for workcell coordination.  p   figure id  attachment_27439  class  wp-caption aligncenter  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27439    div id  attachment_27439  style  width  766px  class  wp-caption aligncenter   img loading  lazy  decoding  async  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27439  class  wp-image-27439  src    sizes  auto,  max-width  756px  100vw, 756px  srcset    1024w,   300w,   768w,   1536w,   2008w  alt    width  756  height  534     p id  caption-attachment-27439  class  wp-caption-text  This SPU recirculating pallet conveyor is suitable for in-line accumulating applications. This version shown here is a single-wide variation for buffering areas and compensating for varying cycle times upstream and downstream. Source  mk North America  p   div   figure   p  Where we see a greater increase in collaborative robots is in smaller operations   less than 1,000 employees   where the automation work is being done in-house,  said automation distributor BRAAS Co. product specialist Dan Toynton. BRAAS works closely with several automation manufacturers in designing custom integrations of conveyors and robotics for the packaging industry.  p   p The process of conveyor-robot integration begins with robotic manufacturers   often using their software for installation and programming. Then OEMs or plant engineers can piggyback off that design work to build out the workcell.  p   p The software plug-ins of some robot manufacturers can even configure and control conveyors and their accessories. Once the program is installed, conveyors get added to the programming with inputs and outputs for starting, stopping, referencing speeds, and monitoring status. This integration acts as a handshake between the conveyor and robot and even lets operators run multiple conveyors off a  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  controller  a  at one time   and track global performance.  p   figure id  attachment_27440  class  wp-caption aligncenter  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27440    div id  attachment_27440  style  width  1034px  class  wp-caption aligncenter   img loading  lazy  decoding  async  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27440  class  wp-image-27440 size-large  src    alt    width  1024  height  576     p id  caption-attachment-27440  class  wp-caption-text  Pallet-fixture conveyors increasingly common in automotive-component, medical-device, and electronics manufacturing, as well as automated product testing and inspection.   Source  Adobe Stock  p   div   figure   p Robotic applications run with simpler controls and blindly move to workpieces, so they need those workpieces to be in an exact location on the conveyor. But something needs to impart precision to the operation. In many such cases, this comes from a servo-driven conveyor. Industry-typical repeatability for servo-driven belt conveyors is to  0.75 mm at 100 indexes per minute.  p   p Linear-transfer systems based on direct-drive tracks  akin to linear motors stretched into a racetrack conveyance loop  can be 10 times more precise, with  0.075 mm repeatability. But with conveyance this precise, articulated robotic arms aren t the common pairing. Instead, stationary assembly machinery is more typical, as illustrated by this Beckhoff example   p   figure id  attachment_27441  class  wp-caption aligncenter  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27441    div id  attachment_27441  style  width  1034px  class  wp-caption aligncenter   img loading  lazy  decoding  async  aria-describedby  caption-attachment-27441  class  wp-image-27441 size-large  src    alt    width  1024  height  566     p id  caption-attachment-27441  class  wp-caption-text  XTS Linear product transport.   Source  Beckhoff USA  p   div   figure   p Pallet-fixture conveyors  not to be confused with equipment that moves skids  are another solution to ensure workpieces aren t every which way when they re presented to the robotic arm. These are seeing increased adoption and pairing with robotics. That s because such conveyors can be configured or programmed to start and stop at specific intervals to sync with robotic tasks.  p   p If the workpieces on a conveyor are every which way, machine  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  vision  a  or another solution may be needed to determine workpiece location and orientation relative to the robot s coordinate frame.  p   p  strong Editor s note    strong This article was syndicated from  em  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  The Robot Report  a    em sibling site  em  a href    target  _blank  rel  noopener  Design World  a .   em   p   p The post  a href    the peripheral motion systems that complement robotics  a  appeared first on  a href    Robot Report  a .  p

metadata.author: Lisa Eitel
